Rationale_Annotation: A single marginal episode of the source -- Wu Song's killing of his adulterous sister-in-law, in places quoted almost verbatim -- is extracted and reused as the seed plot from which the entire new novel is grown.

The content domain is transformed from chivalric heroism and outlaw violence into the everyday domestic world of desire, money, and social maneuvering -- the founding move of the Chinese novel of manners.
The narrative focus shifts from a rotating ensemble of outlaw heroes to the enclosed interior of a single merchant household.
The flat villainess of the source is given psychological depth and her moral condemnation is suspended, transforming her narrative function from object of judgment to ambiguous subject.
The core cast (Xi Men Qing, Pan Jin Lian, Wu Song) is inherited directly from the source; note that Wu Song, a protagonist there, is radically marginalized here.

The dangerous-male-lead archetype is reused, but the source of danger is relocated from the supernatural (vampirism) to the psychological (childhood trauma); the 'darkness' is retained while its explanatory frame becomes realist.
The content domain is inverted: the source's abstinence theme becomes explicit eroticism as the central narrative material.
The asymmetric dominant-male / submissive-female relational dynamic, together with the heroine's redeem-him-through-love arc, is inherited from the source.
The historical era is replaced (Victorian becomes contemporary London) while the geographical setting is preserved.
The short-story collection is recast as serialized television, expanding episodic cases into season-long arcs.
Holmes's baseline characterization is reused but intensified, reframing eccentric rationalism through a modern clinical idiom.
The Holmes-Watson friendship and cohabitation is inherited intact -- the point of contrast with Elementary's reconfiguration.
Elementary's central creative move: Watson is reconceived as Joan Watson, changing both gender and narrative function from male chronicler to sober companion.
A minor canonical detail (Holmes's drug use) is amplified into the protagonist's defining arc as a recovering addict.
Both era and place are replaced: Victorian London becomes contemporary New York City.
Holmes's signature method of observation and deduction is reused as the recognizable prototype of the protagonist's function.
The structural relationship of the consulting detective assisting the official police is inherited from the source.
The Monkey King's ascetic, predestined divinity is subverted into secular romantic love and a postmodern meditation on fate, recasting a religious pilgrimage as romantic tragicomedy.
The recognizable westward-pilgrimage premise is reused as the scaffold on which the new story is built.
The iconic pilgrims, led by Sun Wukong, are inherited from the source.
A factual geographic travelogue is transformed into a supernatural cosmos of gods and demons fusing Confucian, Buddhist, and Daoist thought.
The historical journey of Xuanzang to obtain scriptures -- its route, destination, and Tang-era spatio-temporal frame -- is inherited from the record.
The historical monk Xuanzang is reused as the prototype for the fictional pilgrim Tang Sanzang.
Having begun as a derivative tribute to its predecessor, it reuses the tomb-raider protagonist archetype as the prototype for its own cast.
The grave-robbing world-building -- ancient tombs, supernatural hazards, and the lore governing them -- is inherited from the source.
The dominant mode is transformed from the predecessor's hardcore exploration-and-folklore adventure toward serialized suspense centered on mystery and the emotional bonds between characters.
Originating as Harry Potter fan fiction, it reuses the 'chosen one' archetype and specific character tensions established in the fan community as prototypes for its new cast.
The hidden-magic premise is relocated from a magical boarding school into an urban-fantasy shadow-world ontology, transforming the rules of the magic system.
The central romantic pairing dynamic carried over from the fan community is inherited without transformation.
The fixed villain is elevated to protagonist and given a sympathetic backstory, deconstructing the good-versus-evil binary and adding reflection on propaganda and prejudice.
The focal point shifts from the heroine Dorothy to the previously marginalized Wicked Witch of the West.
The source novel is adapted into a stage musical.
The Land of Oz, its inhabitants, and named characters are inherited from the source.
The witches of Oz are reused as the retelling's central figures.
Zombie-apocalypse horror is injected into the comedy of manners, producing a parodic cross-genre mash-up.
The plot skeleton and large stretches of the original prose and dialogue are inherited near-verbatim.
The Bennet sisters and other named characters are reused, recast as trained zombie-slayers.
As an authorized continuation, it fills a storyline the parent text left blank (Yun Tianming's fate), expanding the original's cosmic-sociology concepts into new territory.
The physics, cosmology, and world-building of the Three-Body universe are inherited directly from the source.
Canonical characters such as Yun Tianming are reused to suture the continuation to the parent text.
A decade-spanning ancient epic is compressed onto a single ordinary day in modern Dublin.
Narration shifts from external heroic deeds to interior stream-of-consciousness.
The classical epic poem is transformed into a modernist prose novel.
The episodic skeleton of the Homeric source is inherited as a metaphorical scaffold mapping each chapter to an episode.
The hero Odysseus is reimagined as the everyman Leopold Bloom, an imitative mapping of prototype onto modern figure.
The setting is transposed from Renaissance Verona to 1950s New York, recasting aristocratic feud as urban racial and gang conflict.
The verse tragedy is transformed into a song-and-dance musical.
The core relational topology -- two lovers caught between two feuding factions, ending in tragedy -- is inherited from the source.
The lovers Romeo and Juliet are reused as prototypes for Tony and Maria.
The Regency gentry's matchmaking and class hierarchy is mapped onto a 1990s Beverly Hills high school.
The source novel is adapted into a feature film.
The matchmaking plot and its interpersonal power dynamics are inherited and shown to still operate in a modern microcosm.
The heroine Emma Woodhouse is reused as the prototype for Cher Horowitz.
The epistolary novel is transformed into a silent expressionist film whose Gothic amplification shaped later vampire visual conventions.
Lacking authorization, the film files off the source's names -- renaming Dracula as Count Orlok -- while reusing the underlying vampire prototype.
The core plot -- a vampire's relocation and the predatory plague he spreads -- is inherited from the source.
The famous wuxia characters are extracted from the jianghu and relocated into a modern university campus, pioneering the 'characters-in-a-new-context' fan fiction model.
The content domain shifts from martial heroism to everyday campus youth and friendship.
Jin Yong's characters (Guo Jing, Huang Rong, Linghu Chong, and others) are reused as prototypes for the campus cast.
The web of relationships among the original characters is transposed largely intact into the new setting.
The chapter-based mythological novel is reimagined as manga, a transformation influential enough to turn the classical Fengshen IP into a modern repository of fan-creative material.
The fixed moral alignments of the deities are reinterpreted, recasting roles and motivations across the ensemble.
The mythological cosmology of the source is inherited as the world framework.
The deities and immortals of the source, led by Taigong Wang, are reused as the manga's cast.
The verses of Li Bai's poem are reused as the central plot device: martial masters study the text hoping to unlock a legendary skill, yet only an illiterate protagonist grasps its true essence.
A short classical poem is expanded across media into a full-length wuxia novel that interrogates what it means to be truly chivalrous.
The chivalrous spirit and ethos defined by the source poem are inherited -- a relationship of spiritual continuity across literary forms.
